text: A 5-zone intensity scale is the most frequently used framework among endurance practitioners, adopted by 47% of surveyed athletes and coaches.
Norwegian endurance practitioners are 2.7 times more likely to use a 5-zone intensity scale than practitioners from all other regions combined.

Norwegian respondents report the highest %HRmax lower limits for Zones 2 and 3 compared to other regions, indicating higher heart rate thresholds before classifying effort as moderate or hard intensity.
Cyclists self-select the lowest %HRmax lower limits for Zones 1, 2, and 3 compared to other endurance sports, likely due to longer session durations and lower mechanical stress.
Zone demarcations (as %HRmax) for Zones 1-3 differ significantly between running and cycling, with runners using higher heart rate thresholds for the same zone numbers.
Performance level does not significantly affect how practitioners demarcate their intensity zones, suggesting zone frameworks are applied similarly across elite and recreational athletes.

The popularity of 'Zone 2 training' in cycling culture may partly reflect cyclists self-selecting lower zone demarcations, meaning their Zone 2 encompasses a lower absolute intensity range than the same zone label in running.
